metadata.note: This is a gated decision-readiness model. Do NOT average domain scores into a single overall score as the primary output. Decision-readiness status is the primary diagnostic. A readiness index (weighted average) is optional and secondary.

dual_score_architecture.description: Every domain produces two scores: Readiness Score (1-5) and Evidence Confidence Score (0-5). These are always displayed together and never collapsed into a single number.

readiness_score.source: Deterministic rules-based evaluation of intake responses against signal anchors

evidence_confidence_score.source: Evidence level assigned to documents uploaded per signal; averaged across domain signals
evidence_confidence_score.scorer: n8n scoring engine — not Claude. Claude may summarize evidence and flag contradictions only.

levels.1: Self-reported only — intake answer is the only evidence
levels.2: Static document — policy, charter, roadmap, architecture diagram
levels.3: Operating artifact — minutes, completed assessment, signed BAA, workflow diagram in use
levels.4: Quantitative report — KPI dashboard, usage data, audit log, performance metrics
levels.5: Audited or externally validated — third-party audit, certification, FDA clearance

credibility_gap.formula: Credibility Gap = Readiness Score - Evidence Confidence Score
credibility_gap.gating_interaction: A critical gating domain with credibility gap > 1.3 triggers a mandatory validation warning. Ready for RFP status requires Evidence Confidence Score >= 3 across all critical gating domains.

n8n_scores: Evidence confidence level per uploaded document
claude_role: Flag contradictions between self-report and evidence
scorer_responsibility.principle: Claude guides and interprets. n8n scores and decides. Do not let Claude become the scorer.

Red.label: Critical Gap
Red.decision_impact: Blocks decision-readiness. Must resolve before scale or RFP.

Yellow.label: Developing
Yellow.decision_impact: Proceed with defined conditions and remediation plan.

High.definition: Strong evidence: confirmed answers from operator intake and supporting documents
High.threshold: 80% or more of signals have direct evidence
High.note: High confidence scores can be used as-is for investment and procurement decisions.

Medium.definition: Some evidence, some assumptions: mix of confirmed answers and reasonable inference
Medium.note: Medium confidence scores are usable but should be validated before major commitments.

Low.definition: Many missing answers, weak evidence, or unverified claims
Low.threshold: Less than 50% of signals have direct evidence
Low.note: Low confidence scores — even if Green — require validation before use in investment or procurement decisions. The system should flag: 'This domain appears strong but requires validation before being used for major investment or procurement decisions.'

critical_gating_domains.description: If ANY critical gating domain is Red, the overall recommendation CANNOT be Ready for Scale or Ready for RFP, regardless of other domain scores.
critical_gating_domains.red_override_message: One or more critical gating domains are Red. The initiative is not decision-ready for scale or RFP until these are resolved.

secondary_gating_domains.description: If ANY secondary gating domain is Red, the initiative may proceed only as discovery or planning — not full procurement or enterprise deployment.
secondary_gating_domains.red_override_message: One or more secondary gating domains are Red. The initiative may proceed to discovery or planning only — not full procurement or enterprise deployment.

strategic_adoption_domains.description: If these domains are Red, warn that the initiative may lack executive alignment, workflow ownership, or implementation pull-through.
strategic_adoption_domains.red_warning_message: Strategic or adoption domains are Red. The initiative may lack clear executive alignment, workflow ownership, or implementation pull-through. Organizational readiness work is required.

conflict_resolution.rule: Red overrides Green. The lowest-scoring critical gating domain determines the ceiling of the decision-readiness status.

examples.result: Technically feasible, but not decision-ready. Security, privacy, and compliance gaps block movement to scale or RFP.
Strategically attractive, but not investment-ready. The organization should clarify budget envelope and funding ownership before vendor engagement.

examples.scenario: Procurement Readiness = Green, Data Readiness = Red
examples.result: Procurement process may be mature, but requirements are premature because data readiness is unresolved.

1_not_ready.label: Not Ready
trigger_conditions: Any critical gating domain is Red (score < 2.5)
OR two or more domains of any type are Red
1_not_ready.guidance: Foundational work is required before any vendor engagement, RFP, or procurement activity. Identify and resolve Red domain blockers first.

2_ready_internal_alignment.label: Ready for Internal Alignment
trigger_conditions: No critical gating domain is Red
AND significant gaps remain in ownership, governance, data, security, or budget
AND multiple domains are Yellow or one secondary domain is Red
2_ready_internal_alignment.guidance: The concept has promise but major internal alignment work is needed before any external vendor engagement.

3_ready_vendor_discovery.label: Ready for Vendor Discovery
trigger_conditions: No critical gating domain is Red
AND no secondary gating domain is Red
AND the organization has enough clarity to learn from vendors
AND gaps remain that prevent a mature RFP from being issued
3_ready_vendor_discovery.guidance: The organization has sufficient clarity to have structured conversations with vendors and learn what solutions exist — but not enough maturity to issue a binding RFP.

4_ready_targeted_pilot.label: Ready for Targeted Pilot
trigger_conditions: Scope is narrow and well-defined
All critical gating domains are Yellow or Green
Risks are understood and documented
Governance and security requirements are manageable at pilot scale
4_ready_targeted_pilot.guidance: The organization is ready to run a focused, bounded pilot. Governance, security, and data requirements must be explicitly scoped for the pilot — not assumed to extend to full deployment.

5_ready_service_line_scale.label: Ready for Service-Line Scale
trigger_conditions: Clear AI workload defined for a specific department or service line
All critical gating domains are Yellow or Green
Infrastructure is Yellow or Green
Budget is Yellow or Green
Governance is defined at minimum for the target scope
5_ready_service_line_scale.guidance: The organization is ready to deploy AI at department or service-line scale. Enterprise-wide governance may not be required yet, but domain-level governance must be in place.
allowed_next_steps: Integration planning with EHR and data platforms

6_ready_enterprise_planning.label: Ready for Enterprise AI Foundation Planning
trigger_conditions: Strong strategic alignment (Domain 1 Green)
Critical gating domains are Yellow or Green
Organization has sufficient maturity to plan enterprise infrastructure
Not necessarily ready to buy immediately — planning horizon is 6-18 months
6_ready_enterprise_planning.guidance: The organization is ready to design and plan enterprise AI infrastructure — architecture, governance, vendor strategy, and phasing. Procurement may follow after planning is complete.

7_ready_for_rfp.label: Ready for RFP
trigger_conditions: No critical gating domain is Red
No secondary gating domain is Red
Procurement requirements are clearly defined
Governance is formally defined
Budget is realistic and allocated or fundable
Evidence confidence is Medium or High across critical domains
7_ready_for_rfp.guidance: The organization is ready to issue a formal RFP. Requirements must be complete enough to evaluate vendors fairly. Low-confidence scores in any critical domain must be resolved before RFP is issued.
allowed_next_steps: Legal and compliance review of contract terms

scenario_logic.description: Use domain color distribution to select the recommended decision-readiness scenario.

rules.condition: Multiple domains Red, including any critical gating domain
No critical domain Red, most domains Yellow, one or more secondary domain Red
No critical domain Red, no secondary domain Red, most domains Yellow
Narrow scope, all critical domains Yellow or Green, risks documented
Critical domains Green or Yellow, Infrastructure Yellow or Green, Budget Yellow or Green, defined workload
Strong strategic alignment, critical domains Yellow or Green, planning horizon 6-18 months
Most domains Green, High or Medium confidence across critical domains, procurement defined, budget allocated

primary_output_hierarchy: 6. Optional readiness index (weighted average — labeled clearly, placed below status)
per_domain_output: Evidence Confidence Score (0-5 with level label)
Credibility Gap (Readiness Score minus Evidence Confidence Score) with interpretation
Evidence provided (list of documents uploaded for this domain)
Evidence gaps (signals with no supporting evidence)
Contradictions between self-report and evidence (if any)
Standards alignment notes (frameworks mapped to this domain)
Rationale (3-5 sentences, specific to this organization)
Key gaps (2-4 specific named gaps — not categories)
Recommended reference designs for this domain
Reference designs not yet appropriate given current readiness
summary_output: What must be resolved before moving forward
Whether organization is ready for: vendor discovery / pilot / service-line scale / enterprise planning / RFP
Recommended reference designs (from vendor reference library)
Reference designs not ready yet (with explanation)
90-day roadmap with evidence collection targets per domain
RFP requirement starter language by domain
output_specification.false_precision_rule: Do NOT generate a single percentage score as the primary finding. If a readiness index is included, label it explicitly as a secondary, informational metric. Primary output is always decision-readiness status.
output_specification.conservative_bias_rule: The scoring model should be conservative — especially when evidence confidence is Low or when regulated healthcare risks are present. When in doubt, recommend the lower decision-readiness status with a clear remediation path.

domain_1.name: Strategic Intent & Use Case Scope
domain_1.description: Clarity of AI ambition, specificity of use cases, executive mandate, and alignment between stated intent and organizational capacity.

s1_1.name: Use Case Specificity
s1_1.description: How clearly defined are the AI use cases driving this initiative?

score_anchors.1: AI interest is general or vendor-driven. No specific use cases defined. 'We want to do something with AI.'
score_anchors.3: 1-2 use cases named but not fully scoped. Rationale exists but success criteria are vague.
score_anchors.5: Use cases are clearly defined, scoped, and tied to measurable clinical or operational outcomes.

s1_2.name: Executive Mandate Clarity
s1_2.description: Is AI investment being driven by a board or CEO mandate, or is it primarily vendor pressure or internal curiosity?

score_anchors.1: No executive mandate. Interest is informal or vendor-initiated.
score_anchors.3: Executive interest exists but mandate is informal. No named accountable leader.
score_anchors.5: Formal executive mandate with named sponsor, defined timeline, and board-level visibility.

s1_3.name: Ambition-Capacity Alignment
s1_3.description: Does the stated AI ambition match the organization's current governance, data, and infrastructure capacity?

score_anchors.1: Enterprise AI ambition with pilot-level readiness. Significant misalignment.
score_anchors.3: Ambition is roughly aligned with capacity, with identifiable gaps that are being addressed.
score_anchors.5: Ambition is calibrated to capacity. Phased plan exists to grow capability alongside ambition.

s1_4.name: Success Metrics Defined
s1_4.description: Are measurable outcomes defined for the AI initiative?

score_anchors.1: No success metrics. 'We'll know it when we see it.'
score_anchors.3: Qualitative outcomes described but not quantified or tied to measurement mechanisms.
score_anchors.5: Specific, measurable, time-bound success criteria defined and agreed by stakeholders.

s1_5.name: Strategic Urgency and Timeline
s1_5.description: Is there a defined timeline and sense of urgency that reflects realistic implementation capacity?

score_anchors.1: No timeline. Urgency is board pressure with no operational plan behind it.
score_anchors.3: General timeline exists but milestones are not defined or resourced.
score_anchors.5: Phased timeline with milestones, resource allocation, and governance gate checkpoints.

domain_2.name: Data Readiness & Governance
domain_2.description: Availability, quality, and governance of data required to support AI workloads. Includes EHR integration, data platform maturity, and interoperability posture.

s2_1.name: Data Availability
s2_1.description: Is the data required for the defined AI use cases available and accessible?

score_anchors.1: Required data does not exist, is inaccessible, or is owned by a vendor without data sharing provisions.
score_anchors.3: Data exists but is partially accessible, fragmented across systems, or requires significant transformation.
score_anchors.5: Required data is available, accessible, and in a format usable for AI without major transformation.

s2_2.name: Data Quality
s2_2.description: Is clinical and operational data reliable, complete, and consistent?

score_anchors.1: Known data quality issues — incomplete records, inconsistent coding, unreliable fields relevant to AI workloads.
score_anchors.3: Data quality is adequate in some domains but has known gaps in areas relevant to the AI use case.
score_anchors.5: Data quality has been formally assessed and meets the threshold required for AI model training and inference.

s2_3.name: Interoperability
s2_3.description: Can data flow between systems required for the AI workload — EHR, data warehouse, AI platform, and downstream consumers?

score_anchors.1: Significant interoperability gaps. Data is siloed. FHIR or HL7 integration is absent or immature.
score_anchors.3: Some integration capability exists. FHIR or API connectivity partially in place but not comprehensive.
score_anchors.5: Strong interoperability posture. FHIR R4 or equivalent APIs in place. Data flows reliably across required systems.

s2_4.name: Data Governance Maturity
s2_4.description: Is there a formal data governance structure with ownership, policies, and enforcement?

score_anchors.1: No data governance program. Data ownership is unclear. No formal data policies.
score_anchors.3: Data governance exists informally or partially. Policies exist but adoption is inconsistent.
score_anchors.5: Mature data governance program with named data owners, formal policies, audit processes, and regular review.

s2_5.name: EHR Platform Readiness
s2_5.description: Is the EHR platform capable of supporting the AI workloads — through native AI tools, marketplace integrations, or API connectivity?

score_anchors.1: EHR platform is a limiting factor. Legacy system, poor API support, or vendor constraints block AI integration.
score_anchors.3: EHR platform can support AI with effort. Some API capability. Vendor AI marketplace accessible but not activated.
score_anchors.5: EHR platform is AI-ready. Native AI tools licensed and in use or planned. APIs are current and well-documented.

s2_6.name: Data Platform Maturity
s2_6.description: Does a centralized data warehouse, data lake, or analytics platform exist to support AI workloads?

score_anchors.1: No centralized data platform. Analytics is report-based. No platform for AI model training or inference at scale.
score_anchors.3: Data platform exists but is not optimized for AI. Limited compute, partial data coverage, or governance gaps.
score_anchors.5: Mature, governed data platform with sufficient compute, broad data coverage, and AI workload capability.

domain_3.name: Infrastructure & Architecture Readiness
domain_3.description: Compute, cloud, network, edge, and integration infrastructure capacity to support AI workloads at the intended scale.

s3_1.name: Compute Availability
s3_1.description: Is dedicated compute capacity available for AI workloads without competing with production clinical systems?

score_anchors.1: No dedicated AI compute. AI workloads would compete directly with clinical production systems.
score_anchors.3: Some compute available but not dedicated or not sized for the intended AI workload.
score_anchors.5: Dedicated AI compute provisioned or committed. Sized appropriately for intended workloads with headroom for growth.

s3_2.name: Cloud Strategy and Maturity
s3_2.description: Is the organization's cloud strategy defined and mature enough to support AI infrastructure needs?

score_anchors.1: No defined cloud strategy. Infrastructure is entirely on-premises with no migration plan.
score_anchors.3: Cloud strategy exists. Partial cloud adoption. AI workloads would require new cloud capacity.
score_anchors.5: Cloud-first or cloud-ready strategy. AI-relevant workloads are or can be cloud-hosted with governance in place.

s3_3.name: Network Readiness
s3_3.description: Is the network infrastructure capable of supporting AI workloads — bandwidth, latency, and segmentation?

score_anchors.1: Network is a known constraint. Bandwidth, latency, or segmentation issues would impact AI workload performance.
score_anchors.3: Network is adequate for current workloads but would require upgrades for AI at scale.
score_anchors.5: Network infrastructure is AI-ready. Segmentation, bandwidth, and latency requirements are met for intended workloads.

s3_4.name: Integration Architecture
s3_4.description: Is the integration layer mature enough to connect AI tools to EHR, data platforms, and clinical workflows?

score_anchors.1: Point-to-point integrations only. No integration platform or API layer. Each new AI tool requires custom integration.
score_anchors.3: Integration platform exists but coverage is incomplete. Some API capability but not standardized.
score_anchors.5: Mature integration platform with standardized APIs (FHIR, REST, HL7). AI tools can be connected through documented interfaces.

s3_5.name: Scalability and Resilience
s3_5.description: Can the infrastructure scale with AI workload growth and maintain resilience for AI-dependent clinical workflows?

score_anchors.1: Infrastructure is brittle. Scaling requires significant capital investment. No DR plan for AI-dependent workflows.
score_anchors.3: Infrastructure can scale with effort. DR plan exists for core systems but not specifically for AI-dependent workflows.
score_anchors.5: Infrastructure scales elastically. DR and BCP plans explicitly cover AI-dependent clinical workflows.

domain_4.name: Security, Privacy & Compliance
domain_4.description: CISO involvement, third-party risk management, cybersecurity posture, regulatory compliance, and vendor data handling requirements.

s4_1.name: CISO Involvement in AI Decisions
s4_1.description: Is the CISO actively involved in AI procurement and deployment decisions?

score_anchors.1: CISO is not involved in AI decisions. AI tools have been or could be deployed without security review.
score_anchors.3: CISO is aware and sometimes consulted but not formally integrated into AI procurement governance.
score_anchors.5: CISO is a formal stakeholder in AI governance. Security review is a required gate in the AI approval process.

s4_2.name: Third-Party Risk Management
s4_2.description: Is there a formal TPRM process that applies to AI vendors?

score_anchors.1: No formal TPRM process. AI vendors are procured without structured security assessment.
score_anchors.3: TPRM process exists for IT vendors generally but has not been adapted for AI-specific risks.
score_anchors.5: Mature TPRM process explicitly covers AI vendors — including model governance, data handling, and ongoing monitoring.

s4_3.name: Cybersecurity Incident Posture
s4_3.description: Cybersecurity incident history and current recovery posture relevant to AI deployment risk.

score_anchors.1: Recent significant incident (within 24 months) with unresolved exposure or ongoing remediation. High-risk posture.
score_anchors.3: Incident history exists but remediation is substantially complete. Posture is improving but not fully hardened.
score_anchors.5: No recent significant incidents. Strong proactive posture. Incident response plan is tested and current.

s4_4.name: Regulatory Compliance Posture
s4_4.description: HIPAA, state privacy regulations, and applicable FDA AI/ML guidance compliance posture.

score_anchors.1: Compliance posture is unclear or known gaps exist in HIPAA, state privacy, or FDA requirements relevant to AI.
score_anchors.3: Compliance posture is adequate for current operations but has not been specifically assessed for AI workloads.
score_anchors.5: Compliance has been formally assessed for AI use cases. Legal and compliance have reviewed AI deployment requirements.

s4_5.name: AI Vendor Data Handling Requirements
s4_5.description: Are data handling, retention, and model training restrictions defined and imposed on AI vendors in contracts?

score_anchors.1: No defined requirements. Vendor data handling is accepted as-written in vendor contracts.
score_anchors.3: General data handling requirements exist in IT contracts. AI-specific requirements are not yet defined.
score_anchors.5: AI-specific data handling requirements are defined: no training on patient data, retention limits, data residency, audit rights.

s4_6.name: Security Requirements in Vendor Contracts
s4_6.description: Are security, audit, and breach notification requirements formally imposed on AI vendors?

score_anchors.1: Vendor contracts do not include security, audit, or breach notification requirements specific to AI.
score_anchors.3: Standard IT security requirements are in contracts. AI-specific requirements (model explainability, security testing) are not yet defined.
score_anchors.5: Contracts include AI-specific security requirements: penetration testing, model audit rights, breach notification SLAs, data residency.

domain_5.name: AI Governance & Operating Model
domain_5.description: Existence and maturity of AI governance structures, accountability for AI performance and harms, operating model for post-deployment ownership, and model inventory management.

s5_1.name: AI Governance Body
s5_1.description: Does a formal AI governance committee or equivalent decision authority exist?

score_anchors.1: No AI governance body. AI decisions are made ad hoc by whoever is involved in a given project.
score_anchors.3: AI governance body exists informally or is in formation. Meets irregularly. No formal authority to block or approve AI tools.
score_anchors.5: Formal AI governance committee with defined authority, regular cadence, multi-stakeholder representation, and documented charter.

s5_2.name: AI Approval Process
s5_2.description: Is there a defined process for approving new AI tools before deployment?

score_anchors.1: No approval process. AI tools are deployed at department level without central review.
score_anchors.3: Approval process is informally defined. IT is generally consulted but clinical, compliance, and governance review is inconsistent.
score_anchors.5: Formal approval process with defined stages: clinical review, security review, governance sign-off, post-deployment monitoring trigger.

s5_3.name: AI Tool Inventory
s5_3.description: Does the organization maintain an inventory of AI tools currently in use?

score_anchors.1: No inventory. Unknown number of AI tools in use across the organization.
score_anchors.3: Partial inventory. IT-purchased tools are tracked but department-level AI tools may not be visible.
score_anchors.5: Comprehensive, current AI tool inventory with vendor, use case, data inputs, clinical scope, and governance status for each tool.

s5_4.name: Accountability for AI Performance and Harms
s5_4.description: Is there a named owner accountable when an AI tool performs incorrectly or causes a harm?

score_anchors.1: No defined accountability. When an AI tool fails, there is no clear process or named owner.
score_anchors.3: General accountability exists at the IT or clinical informatics level but is not formalized per tool.
score_anchors.5: Named accountability per AI tool with documented escalation path and harm reporting process tied to quality and safety programs.

s5_5.name: Post-Deployment Monitoring
s5_5.description: Does a process exist for monitoring AI tools after go-live?

score_anchors.1: No post-deployment monitoring. Tools are deployed and assumed to perform correctly.
score_anchors.3: Informal monitoring. Issues are addressed reactively. No systematic performance tracking.
score_anchors.5: Formal post-deployment monitoring process with defined metrics, review cadence, escalation triggers, and vendor accountability.

s5_6.name: Operating Model Clarity
s5_6.description: Is there a defined operating model for AI post-deployment — who owns it, who trains staff, who manages the vendor relationship?

score_anchors.1: No operating model. Deployment creates orphaned AI tools with no clear owner post-go-live.
score_anchors.3: Operating model is partially defined. IT owns tools generally but clinical and operational ownership is unclear.
score_anchors.5: Fully defined operating model: named tool owner, staff training program, vendor management accountabilities, and review calendar.

domain_6.name: Workflow Adoption & Change Readiness
domain_6.description: Clinical and operational stakeholder engagement, change management capacity, executive sponsorship, and organizational track record with technology adoption.

s6_1.name: Clinical Stakeholder Engagement
s6_1.description: Are clinical leaders — CMIO, clinical informatics, department chiefs — actively involved in AI use case selection and validation?

score_anchors.1: Clinical stakeholders are not engaged. AI decisions are made by IT or administration without clinical input.
score_anchors.3: Clinical informatics is consulted. Clinical leaders are aware but not actively co-designing use cases.
score_anchors.5: Clinical champions are co-owners of use case definition and validation. CMIO or equivalent is a formal governance participant.

s6_2.name: Change Management Capacity
s6_2.description: Does the organization have the capacity to drive technology adoption effectively?

score_anchors.1: Technology adoption is a known organizational weakness. Past implementations have had low adoption.
score_anchors.3: Change management capability exists but is inconsistently applied. Results vary by project and department.
score_anchors.5: Strong change management capability with a defined process, dedicated resources, and proven track record on recent implementations.

s6_3.name: Executive Sponsorship
s6_3.description: Is there a named executive sponsor with authority and accountability for this AI initiative?

score_anchors.1: No named executive sponsor. Initiative is IT-led without executive cover.
score_anchors.3: Executive awareness exists but sponsorship is passive. No named accountable executive.
score_anchors.5: Named executive sponsor with authority to allocate resources, resolve roadblocks, and communicate priority to the organization.

s6_4.name: Workflow Integration Plan
s6_4.description: Is there a plan for integrating AI tools into clinical or operational workflows, not just deploying them?

score_anchors.1: No workflow integration plan. Assumption is that staff will adapt to the tool once deployed.
score_anchors.3: General plan exists for training and rollout but workflow redesign has not been addressed.
score_anchors.5: Workflow integration has been mapped. Clinical workflow redesign, training plan, and adoption metrics are defined.

s6_5.name: Hard Constraint Burden
s6_5.description: Are there organizational constraints — financial pressure, EHR migration, regulatory investigation, leadership change — that could derail AI adoption?

score_anchors.1: Multiple hard constraints active simultaneously. AI initiative is competing with existential organizational priorities.
score_anchors.3: One or two constraints exist but are being managed. AI initiative has organizational space to proceed with care.
score_anchors.5: No significant hard constraints. Organizational capacity is available for AI initiative execution.

domain_7.name: Procurement & Vendor Readiness
domain_7.description: Maturity of the procurement process, clarity of AI requirements before vendor engagement, vendor evaluation competency, and governance gates in the procurement process.

s7_1.name: RFP Process Maturity
s7_1.description: Does a defined RFP or procurement process exist for technology at this scale?

score_anchors.1: No defined procurement process for AI or complex technology. Process is invented case by case.
score_anchors.3: General IT procurement process exists. Not specifically designed for AI or AI infrastructure scale.
score_anchors.5: Mature procurement process for complex technology. Experience with multi-vendor evaluations. Governance gates are defined.

s7_2.name: Requirements Definition Maturity
s7_2.description: How well-defined are AI requirements before vendor conversations begin?

score_anchors.1: Requirements are not defined. Vendor conversations are happening before internal requirements are clear.
score_anchors.3: High-level requirements exist but are not specific enough to evaluate vendors fairly or write binding RFP language.
score_anchors.5: Specific, measurable requirements are defined across functional, technical, security, data, and governance dimensions.

s7_3.name: Vendor Evaluation Competency
s7_3.description: Does the organization have the competency to evaluate AI vendors — scoring criteria, proof-of-concept design, reference checks?

score_anchors.1: No structured vendor evaluation process. Decisions are based on demos and vendor relationships.
score_anchors.3: Evaluation criteria exist in general terms. POC experience is limited. Reference check process is informal.
score_anchors.5: Structured vendor evaluation with defined scoring criteria, POC requirements, technical due diligence, and formal reference check process.

s7_4.name: AI Procurement Experience
s7_4.description: Has the organization run an AI-specific RFP before? What was learned?

score_anchors.1: No AI procurement experience. Significant learning curve anticipated.
score_anchors.3: Limited AI procurement experience. One or two prior AI acquisitions, informally managed.
score_anchors.5: Multiple AI procurements completed. Lessons learned are documented. Requirements have been refined from prior experience.

s7_5.name: Governance Gates in Procurement
s7_5.description: Are there formal governance approval gates before an AI contract is signed?

score_anchors.1: No governance gates. Contracts can be signed without AI governance, clinical, or compliance review.
score_anchors.3: Legal and IT review contracts but AI governance and clinical compliance review are not formal requirements.
score_anchors.5: Formal governance gates before signature: AI governance approval, CISO sign-off, clinical compliance review, legal review.

domain_8.name: Budget & Investment Readiness
domain_8.description: Realism and availability of budget for the AI initiative, awareness of total cost of ownership, alignment between investment expectations and organizational ambition, and financial constraints.

s8_1.name: Budget Availability
s8_1.description: Is a realistic budget allocated, approved, or fundable for this AI initiative?

score_anchors.1: No budget identified or allocated. Investment expectations are aspirational with no funding path.
score_anchors.3: Budget is directionally identified but not formally approved or fully allocated.
score_anchors.5: Budget is approved, allocated, and realistic for the scope of the initiative.

s8_2.name: Budget-Ambition Alignment
s8_2.description: Does the stated budget match the stated AI ambition?

score_anchors.1: Significant mismatch. Enterprise AI ambition with pilot-level budget. Expectation gap is a project risk.
score_anchors.3: Budget and ambition are roughly aligned with some gaps. Scope adjustment may be needed.
score_anchors.5: Budget is fully aligned with ambition. Investment envelope covers infrastructure, implementation, training, and ongoing operations.

s8_3.name: Total Cost of Ownership Awareness
s8_3.description: Is there awareness of TCO beyond licensing — implementation, integration, training, ongoing operations, and support?

score_anchors.1: Budget thinking is limited to licensing fees. Implementation, integration, and operational costs are not modeled.
score_anchors.3: TCO awareness exists at a general level. Some implementation and training costs are factored in but not fully modeled.
score_anchors.5: Full TCO model including licensing, implementation, integration, training, ongoing operations, support, and refresh cycle.

s8_4.name: Financial Constraint Burden
s8_4.description: Are financial pressures — margin compression, capital constraints, competing priorities — limiting AI investment capacity?

score_anchors.1: Significant financial pressure. AI investment is competing with operational survival priorities. High risk of de-prioritization.
score_anchors.3: Financial pressure exists but AI initiative has organizational support. Budget is at risk if financial situation worsens.
score_anchors.5: Financial position supports AI investment. Budget is protected from margin or capital pressures.

s8_5.name: ROI and Value Expectations
s8_5.description: Are there defined expectations for financial or operational return on AI investment?

score_anchors.1: No ROI framework. Investment is made on faith or board pressure without defined value expectations.
score_anchors.3: General value expectations exist (efficiency, quality improvement) but are not quantified or tied to measurement.
score_anchors.5: Defined ROI framework with quantified expectations, measurement methodology, and defined review timeline.
